{{ill|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|櫛名田比売命}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐能袁命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto|ja|天之菩卑命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto|ja|天之忍穂耳命}}, {{ill|Amatsuhikone-no-Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命}}, {{ill|Ikutsuhikone-no-Mikoto|ja|活津日子根命}}, {{ill|Kumano-Kusubi-no-Mikoto|ja|熊野久須比命}}, {{ill|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|ja|市寸島比売命}}, {{ill|Takiribime-no-Mikoto|ja|多紀理比売命}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto|ja|多岐津比売命}}
South Hall:
{{ill|Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|ja|大国主}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto|ja|少比古那命}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命}}, {{ill|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|櫛名田比売命}} | type = [[Ken-sha]] (as of May 15, [[Meiji]] 6) | Established = By {{ill|Hosokawa Tadaoki|ja|細川忠興}} in [[Genna (era)|Genna]] 3 (1617), February | Main Hall = Nagare-zukuri style | Festival = Gion Festival ([[Kokura Gion Daiko]]) on the third Saturday and Sunday of July | Rituals = }} [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan03s3.jpg|thumb|Haiden (Worship Hall)]] [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan08s3.jpg|thumb|Main Hall]] [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan09s3.jpg|thumb|East Gate]] '''Kokura Gion Yasaka Shrine''' (こくらぎおんやさかじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in {{ill|Jōnai|ja|城内 (北九州市)}}, [[Kokurakita Ward]], [[Kitakyushu]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]]. It enshrines deities as listed in the template above. Its previous ranking under the [[Modern System of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] was as a [[Ken-sha]] (Prefectural Shrine). == Overview == Historically called “Kokura no Gion-san,” the shrine is known for the {{ill|Kokura Gion Taiko|ja|小倉祇園太鼓}} festival depicted in the film adaptations of ''{{ill|The Life of Matsu the Untamed|ja|無法松の一生}}'' featuring actors like {{ill|Bando Tsumasaburō|ja|阪東妻三郎}} (1943 version) and {{ill|Mifune Toshirō|ja|三船敏郎}} (1958 version). The festival, first celebrated in [[Genna (era)|Genna]] 4 (1618), has over 390 years of history and now takes place annually on the third Saturday and Sunday of July. The shrine attracts the largest number of visitors in Kitakyushu and the surrounding areas. Initially called {{ill|Gion-sha|ja|祇園社}}, the shrine’s name was changed to Yasaka Shrine during the [[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto-Buddhism Separation Order]] of [[Meiji]] 4 (1868), which resulted in the broader {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}} movement. The shrine is currently an independent entity, unaffiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. == Timeline == * {{ill|Jōgan (era)|ja|貞観}} 12 (870), April – To combat an epidemic, the {{ill|Gion-sha|ja|祇園社}} from Yasaka in [[Yamashiro Province]] was enshrined at Nagao (near present-day {{ill|Nagao, Kokuraminami|ja|長尾 (小倉南区)}}), becoming the regional guardian of {{ill|Kiku District|ja|企救郡}}. * [[Keichō]] 7 (1602) – {{ill|Hosokawa Tadaoki|ja|細川忠興}} begins constructing [[Kokura Castle]]. * [[Genna (era)|Genna]] 3 (1617) – The shrine is moved to its current location in Kokura Castle’s northern grounds as the clan guardian shrine. The southern and northern halls house deities transferred from Nagao and Sambommatsu (present-day {{ill|Tanga Market|ja|旦過市場}}), respectively. * Genna 4 (1618) – Inspired by the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|ja|祇園祭}} in Kyoto, Hosokawa introduces the Kokura Gion Festival, initially featuring decorated floats. * [[Meiji]] 6 (1873), May 15 – Designated a Ken-sha. * [[Shōwa]] 18 (1943), October 28 – The release of ''The Life of Matsu the Untamed'' popularizes the Kokura Gion Daiko nationally. * Shōwa 33 (1958), April 3 – Kokura Gion Daiko is designated an [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property]] by Fukuoka Prefecture. * Shōwa 62 (1987) – The current festival schedule is established. == Access == * A 4-minute (350 m) walk from [[Nishikokura Station]]. * Approximately 1.5 km from the [[Kitakyushu Urban Expressway]]’s {{ill|Ōtemachi Interchange|ja|大手町出入口}} and 1.1 km from {{ill|Katsuyama Interchange|ja|勝山出入口}}. == Nearby Attractions == * [[Kokura Castle]] * {{ill|Riverwalk Kitakyushu|ja|リバーウォーク北九州}} * [[Kitakyushu City Hall]] * {{ill|Katsuyama Park (Kitakyushu)|ja|勝山公園 (北九州市)}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Yasaka-jinja (Kitakyushu)}} * [http://www.yasaka-jinja.com/ Official Website of Yasaka Shrine] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{citation needed|Yasaka Shrine Kitakyushu}} [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines of Fukuoka]] [[Category:Shrines in {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan02s3.jpg|300px]] | name = Yasaka Shrine | location = [[Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka]], [[Kitakyushu]], [[Kokurakita Ward]], {{ill|Jōnai|ja|城内 (北九州市)}} 2-2 | coordinates = {{Coord|33|53|07.68|N|130|52|25.39|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Yasaka Shrine}} | Deities = North Hall:
{{ill|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|櫛名田比売命}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐能袁命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto|ja|天之菩卑命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto|ja|天之忍穂耳命}}, {{ill|Amatsuhikone-no-Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命}}, {{ill|Ikutsuhikone-no-Mikoto|ja|活津日子根命}}, {{ill|Kumano-Kusubi-no-Mikoto|ja|熊野久須比命}}, {{ill|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|ja|市寸島比売命}}, {{ill|Takiribime-no-Mikoto|ja|多紀理比売命}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto|ja|多岐津比売命}}
South Hall:
{{ill|Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|ja|大国主}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto|ja|少比古那命}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命}}, {{ill|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|櫛名田比売命}} | type = [[Ken-sha]] (as of May 15, [[Meiji]] 6) | Established = By {{ill|Hosokawa Tadaoki|ja|細川忠興}} in [[Genna (era)|Genna]] 3 (1617), February | Main Hall = Nagare-zukuri style | Festival = Gion Festival ([[Kokura Gion Daiko]]) on the third Saturday and Sunday of July | Rituals = }} [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan03s3.jpg|thumb|Haiden (Worship Hall)]] [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan08s3.jpg|thumb|Main Hall]] [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan09s3.jpg|thumb|East Gate]] '''Kokura Gion Yasaka Shrine''' (こくらぎおんやさかじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in {{ill|Jōnai|ja|城内 (北九州市)}}, [[Kokurakita Ward]], [[Kitakyushu]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]]. It enshrines deities as listed in the template above. Its previous ranking under the [[Modern System of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] was as a [[Ken-sha]] (Prefectural Shrine). == Overview == Historically called “Kokura no Gion-san,” the shrine is known for the {{ill|Kokura Gion Taiko|ja|小倉祇園太鼓}} festival depicted in the film adaptations of ''{{ill|The Life of Matsu the Untamed|ja|無法松の一生}}'' featuring actors like {{ill|Bando Tsumasaburō|ja|阪東妻三郎}} (1943 version) and {{ill|Mifune Toshirō|ja|三船敏郎}} (1958 version). The festival, first celebrated in [[Genna (era)|Genna]] 4 (1618), has over 390 years of history and now takes place annually on the third Saturday and Sunday of July. The shrine attracts the largest number of visitors in Kitakyushu and the surrounding areas. Initially called {{ill|Gion-sha|ja|祇園社}}, the shrine’s name was changed to Yasaka Shrine during the [[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto-Buddhism Separation Order]] of [[Meiji]] 4 (1868), which resulted in the broader {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}} movement. The shrine is currently an independent entity, unaffiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. == Timeline == * {{ill|Jōgan (era)|ja|貞観}} 12 (870), April – To combat an epidemic, the {{ill|Gion-sha|ja|祇園社}} from Yasaka in [[Yamashiro Province]] was enshrined at Nagao (near present-day {{ill|Nagao, Kokuraminami|ja|長尾 (小倉南区)}}), becoming the regional guardian of {{ill|Kiku District|ja|企救郡}}. * [[Keichō]] 7 (1602) – {{ill|Hosokawa Tadaoki|ja|細川忠興}} begins constructing [[Kokura Castle]]. * [[Genna (era)|Genna]] 3 (1617) – The shrine is moved to its current location in Kokura Castle’s northern grounds as the clan guardian shrine. The southern and northern halls house deities transferred from Nagao and Sambommatsu (present-day {{ill|Tanga Market|ja|旦過市場}}), respectively. * Genna 4 (1618) – Inspired by the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|ja|祇園祭}} in Kyoto, Hosokawa introduces the Kokura Gion Festival, initially featuring decorated floats. * [[Meiji]] 6 (1873), May 15 – Designated a Ken-sha. * [[Shōwa]] 18 (1943), October 28 – The release of ''The Life of Matsu the Untamed'' popularizes the Kokura Gion Daiko nationally. * Shōwa 33 (1958), April 3 – Kokura Gion Daiko is designated an [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property]] by Fukuoka Prefecture. * Shōwa 62 (1987) – The current festival schedule is established. == Access == * A 4-minute (350 m) walk from [[Nishikokura Station]]. * Approximately 1.5 km from the [[Kitakyushu Urban Expressway]]’s {{ill|Ōtemachi Interchange|ja|大手町出入口}} and 1.1 km from {{ill|Katsuyama Interchange|ja|勝山出入口}}. == Nearby Attractions == * [[Kokura Castle]] * {{ill|Riverwalk Kitakyushu|ja|リバーウォーク北九州}} * [[Kitakyushu City Hall]] * {{ill|Katsuyama Park (Kitakyushu)|ja|勝山公園 (北九州市)}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Yasaka-jinja (Kitakyushu)}} * [http://www.yasaka-jinja.com/ Official Website of Yasaka Shrine] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{citation needed|Yasaka Shrine Kitakyushu}} [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines of Fukuoka]] [[Category:Shrines in {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan02s3.jpg|300px]] | name = Yasaka Shrine | location = [[Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka]], [[Kitakyushu]], [[Kokurakita Ward]], {{ill|Jōnai|ja|城内 (北九州市)}} 2-2 | coordinates = {{Coord|33|53|07.68|N|130|52|25.39|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Yasaka Shrine}} | Deities = North Hall:
{{ill|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|櫛名田比売命}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐能袁命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto|ja|天之菩卑命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto|ja|天之忍穂耳命}}, {{ill|Amatsuhikone-no-Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命}}, {{ill|Ikutsuhikone-no-Mikoto|ja|活津日子根命}}, {{ill|Kumano-Kusubi-no-Mikoto|ja|熊野久須比命}}, {{ill|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|ja|市寸島比売命}}, {{ill|Takiribime-no-Mikoto|ja|多紀理比売命}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto|ja|多岐津比売命}}
South Hall:
{{ill|Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|ja|大国主}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto|ja|少比古那命}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命}}, {{ill|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|櫛名田比売命}} | type = [[Ken-sha]] (as of May 15, [[Meiji]] 6) | Established = By {{ill|Hosokawa Tadaoki|ja|細川忠興}} in [[Genna (era)|Genna]] 3 (1617), February | Main Hall = Nagare-zukuri style | Festival = Gion Festival ([[Kokura Gion Daiko]]) on the third Saturday and Sunday of July | Rituals = }} [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan03s3.jpg|thumb|Haiden (Worship Hall)]] [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan08s3.jpg|thumb|Main Hall]] [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan09s3.jpg|thumb|East Gate]] '''Kokura Gion Yasaka Shrine''' (こくらぎおんやさかじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in {{ill|Jōnai|ja|城内 (北九州市)}}, [[Kokurakita Ward]], [[Kitakyushu]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]]. It enshrines deities as listed in the template above. Its previous ranking under the [[Modern System of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] was as a [[Ken-sha]] (Prefectural Shrine). == Overview == Historically called “Kokura no Gion-san,” the shrine is known for the {{ill|Kokura Gion Taiko|ja|小倉祇園太鼓}} festival depicted in the film adaptations of ''{{ill|The Life of Matsu the Untamed|ja|無法松の一生}}'' featuring actors like {{ill|Bando Tsumasaburō|ja|阪東妻三郎}} (1943 version) and {{ill|Mifune Toshirō|ja|三船敏郎}} (1958 version). The festival, first celebrated in [[Genna (era)|Genna]] 4 (1618), has over 390 years of history and now takes place annually on the third Saturday and Sunday of July. The shrine attracts the largest number of visitors in Kitakyushu and the surrounding areas. Initially called {{ill|Gion-sha|ja|祇園社}}, the shrine’s name was changed to Yasaka Shrine during the [[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto-Buddhism Separation Order]] of [[Meiji]] 4 (1868), which resulted in the broader {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}} movement. The shrine is currently an independent entity, unaffiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. == Timeline == * {{ill|Jōgan (era)|ja|貞観}} 12 (870), April – To combat an epidemic, the {{ill|Gion-sha|ja|祇園社}} from Yasaka in [[Yamashiro Province]] was enshrined at Nagao (near present-day {{ill|Nagao, Kokuraminami|ja|長尾 (小倉南区)}}), becoming the regional guardian of {{ill|Kiku District|ja|企救郡}}. * [[Keichō]] 7 (1602) – {{ill|Hosokawa Tadaoki|ja|細川忠興}} begins constructing [[Kokura Castle]]. * [[Genna (era)|Genna]] 3 (1617) – The shrine is moved to its current location in Kokura Castle’s northern grounds as the clan guardian shrine. The southern and northern halls house deities transferred from Nagao and Sambommatsu (present-day {{ill|Tanga Market|ja|旦過市場}}), respectively. * Genna 4 (1618) – Inspired by the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|ja|祇園祭}} in Kyoto, Hosokawa introduces the Kokura Gion Festival, initially featuring decorated floats. * [[Meiji]] 6 (1873), May 15 – Designated a Ken-sha. * [[Shōwa]] 18 (1943), October 28 – The release of ''The Life of Matsu the Untamed'' popularizes the Kokura Gion Daiko nationally. * Shōwa 33 (1958), April 3 – Kokura Gion Daiko is designated an [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property]] by Fukuoka Prefecture. * Shōwa 62 (1987) – The current festival schedule is established. == Access == * A 4-minute (350 m) walk from [[Nishikokura Station]]. * Approximately 1.5 km from the [[Kitakyushu Urban Expressway]]’s {{ill|Ōtemachi Interchange|ja|大手町出入口}} and 1.1 km from {{ill|Katsuyama Interchange|ja|勝山出入口}}. == Nearby Attractions == * [[Kokura Castle]] * {{ill|Riverwalk Kitakyushu|ja|リバーウォーク北九州}} * [[Kitakyushu City Hall]] * {{ill|Katsuyama Park (Kitakyushu)|ja|勝山公園 (北九州市)}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Yasaka-jinja (Kitakyushu)}} * [http://www.yasaka-jinja.com/ {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan02s3.jpg|300px]] | name = Yasaka Shrine | location = [[Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka]], [[Kitakyushu]], [[Kokurakita Ward]], {{ill|Jōnai|ja|城内 (北九州市)}} 2-2 | coordinates = {{Coord|33|53|07.68|N|130|52|25.39|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Yasaka Shrine}} | Deities = North Hall:
{{ill|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|櫛名田比売命}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐能袁命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto|ja|天之菩卑命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto|ja|天之忍穂耳命}}, {{ill|Amatsuhikone-no-Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命}}, {{ill|Ikutsuhikone-no-Mikoto|ja|活津日子根命}}, {{ill|Kumano-Kusubi-no-Mikoto|ja|熊野久須比命}}, {{ill|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|ja|市寸島比売命}}, {{ill|Takiribime-no-Mikoto|ja|多紀理比売命}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto|ja|多岐津比売命}}
South Hall:
{{ill|Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|ja|大国主}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto|ja|少比古那命}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命}}, {{ill|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|櫛名田比売命}} | type = [[Ken-sha]] (as of May 15, [[Meiji]] 6) | Established = By {{ill|Hosokawa Tadaoki|ja|細川忠興}} in [[Genna (era)|Genna]] 3 (1617), February | Main Hall = Nagare-zukuri style | Festival = Gion Festival ([[Kokura Gion Daiko]]) on the third Saturday and Sunday of July | Rituals = }} [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan03s3.jpg|thumb|Haiden (Worship Hall)]] [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan08s3.jpg|thumb|Main Hall]] [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan09s3.jpg|thumb|East Gate]] '''Kokura Gion Yasaka Shrine''' (こくらぎおんやさかじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in {{ill|Jōnai|ja|城内 (北九州市)}}, [[Kokurakita Ward]], [[Kitakyushu]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]]. It enshrines deities as listed in the template above. Its previous ranking under the [[Modern System of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] was as a [[Ken-sha]] (Prefectural Shrine). == Overview == Historically called “Kokura no Gion-san,” the shrine is known for the {{ill|Kokura Gion Taiko|ja|小倉祇園太鼓}} festival depicted in the film adaptations of ''{{ill|The Life of Matsu the Untamed|ja|無法松の一生}}'' featuring actors like {{ill|Bando Tsumasaburō|ja|阪東妻三郎}} (1943 version) and {{ill|Mifune Toshirō|ja|三船敏郎}} (1958 version). The festival, first celebrated in [[Genna (era)|Genna]] 4 (1618), has over 390 years of history and now takes place annually on the third Saturday and Sunday of July. The shrine attracts the largest number of visitors in Kitakyushu and the surrounding areas. Initially called {{ill|Gion-sha|ja|祇園社}}, the shrine’s name was changed to Yasaka Shrine during the [[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto-Buddhism Separation Order]] of [[Meiji]] 4 (1868), which resulted in the broader {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}} movement. The shrine is currently an independent entity, unaffiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. == Timeline == * {{ill|Jōgan (era)|ja|貞観}} 12 (870), April – To combat an epidemic, the {{ill|Gion-sha|ja|祇園社}} from Yasaka in [[Yamashiro Province]] was enshrined at Nagao (near present-day {{ill|Nagao, Kokuraminami|ja|長尾 (小倉南区)}}), becoming the regional guardian of {{ill|Kiku District|ja|企救郡}}. * [[Keichō]] 7 (1602) – {{ill|Hosokawa Tadaoki|ja|細川忠興}} begins constructing [[Kokura Castle]]. * [[Genna (era)|Genna]] 3 (1617) – The shrine is moved to its current location in Kokura Castle’s northern grounds as the clan guardian shrine. The southern and northern halls house deities transferred from Nagao and Sambommatsu (present-day {{ill|Tanga Market|ja|旦過市場}}), respectively. * Genna 4 (1618) – Inspired by the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|ja|祇園祭}} in Kyoto, Hosokawa introduces the Kokura Gion Festival, initially featuring decorated floats. * [[Meiji]] 6 (1873), May 15 – Designated a Ken-sha. * [[Shōwa]] 18 (1943), October 28 – The release of ''The Life of Matsu the Untamed'' popularizes the Kokura Gion Daiko nationally. * Shōwa 33 (1958), April 3 – Kokura Gion Daiko is designated an [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property]] by Fukuoka Prefecture. * Shōwa 62 (1987) – The current festival schedule is established. == Access == * A 4-minute (350 m) walk from [[Nishikokura Station]]. * Approximately 1.5 km from the [[Kitakyushu Urban Expressway]]’s {{ill|Ōtemachi Interchange|ja|大手町出入口}} and 1.1 km from {{ill|Katsuyama Interchange|ja|勝山出入口}}. == Nearby Attractions == * [[Kokura Castle]] * {{ill|Riverwalk Kitakyushu|ja|リバーウォーク北九州}} * [[Kitakyushu City Hall]] * {{ill|Katsuyama Park (Kitakyushu)|ja|勝山公園 (北九州市)}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Yasaka-jinja (Kitakyushu)}} * [http://www.yasaka-jinja.com/ Official Website of Yasaka Shrine] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{citation needed|Yasaka Shrine Kitakyushu}} [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines of Fukuoka]] [[Category:Shrines in Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Kokurakita Ward History]] [[Category:Kokurakita Ward Architecture]] [[Category:Yasaka Shrines]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yasaka Shrine (Kitakyushu)}}[[Category:Gion Shrines]] {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan02s3.jpg|300px]] | name = Yasaka Shrine | location = [[Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka]], [[Kitakyushu]], [[Kokurakita Ward]], {{ill|Jōnai|ja|城内 (北九州市)}} 2-2 | coordinates = {{Coord|33|53|07.68|N|130|52|25.39|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Yasaka Shrine}} | Deities = North Hall:
{{ill|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|櫛名田比売命}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐能袁命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto|ja|天之菩卑命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto|ja|天之忍穂耳命}}, {{ill|Amatsuhikone-no-Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命|1=Amatsuhikone}}, {{ill|Ikutsuhikone-no-Mikoto|ja|活津日子根命}}, {{ill|Kumano-Kusubi-no-Mikoto|ja|熊野久須比命}}, {{ill|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|ja|市寸島比売命}}, {{ill|Takiribime-no-Mikoto|ja|多紀理比売命}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto|ja|多岐津比売命}}
South Hall:
{{ill|Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|ja|大国主}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto|ja|少比古那命}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命}}, {{ill|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|櫛名田比売命}} | type = [[Ken-sha]] (as of May 15, [[Meiji]] 6) | Established = By {{ill|Hosokawa Tadaoki|ja|細川忠興}} in [[Genna (era)|Genna]] 3 (1617), February | Main Hall = Nagare-zukuri style | Festival = Gion Festival ([[Kokura Gion Daiko]]) on the third Saturday and Sunday of July | Rituals = }} [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan03s3.jpg|thumb|Haiden (Worship Hall)]] [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan08s3.jpg|thumb|Main Hall]] [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan09s3.jpg|thumb|East Gate]] '''Kokura Gion Yasaka Shrine''' (こくらぎおんやさかじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in {{ill|Jōnai|ja|城内 (北九州市)}}, [[Kokurakita Ward]], [[Kitakyushu]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]]. It enshrines deities as listed in the template above. Its previous ranking under the [[Modern System of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] was as a [[Ken-sha]] (Prefectural Shrine). == Overview == Historically called “Kokura no Gion-san,” the shrine is known for the {{ill|Kokura Gion Taiko|ja|小倉祇園太鼓}} festival depicted in the film adaptations of ''{{ill|The Life of Matsu the Untamed|ja|無法松の一生}}'' featuring actors like {{ill|Bando Tsumasaburō|ja|阪東妻三郎}} (1943 version) and {{ill|Mifune Toshirō|ja|三船敏郎}} (1958 version). The festival, first celebrated in [[Genna (era)|Genna]] 4 (1618), has over 390 years of history and now takes place annually on the third Saturday and Sunday of July. The shrine attracts the largest number of visitors in Kitakyushu and the surrounding areas. Initially called {{ill|Gion-sha|ja|祇園社}}, the shrine’s name was changed to Yasaka Shrine during the [[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto-Buddhism Separation Order]] of [[Meiji]] 4 (1868), which resulted in the broader {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}} movement. The shrine is currently an independent entity, unaffiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. == Timeline == * {{ill|Jōgan (era)|ja|貞観}} 12 (870), April – To combat an epidemic, the {{ill|Gion-sha|ja|祇園社}} from Yasaka in [[Yamashiro Province]] was enshrined at Nagao (near present-day {{ill|Nagao, Kokuraminami|ja|長尾 (小倉南区)}}), becoming the regional guardian of {{ill|Kiku District|ja|企救郡}}. * [[Keichō]] 7 (1602) – {{ill|Hosokawa Tadaoki|ja|細川忠興}} begins constructing [[Kokura Castle]]. * [[Genna (era)|Genna]] 3 (1617) – The shrine is moved to its current location in Kokura Castle’s northern grounds as the clan guardian shrine. The southern and northern halls house deities transferred from Nagao and Sambommatsu (present-day {{ill|Tanga Market|ja|旦過市場}}), respectively. * Genna 4 (1618) – Inspired by the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|ja|祇園祭}} in Kyoto, Hosokawa introduces the Kokura Gion Festival, initially featuring decorated floats. * [[Meiji]] 6 (1873), May 15 – Designated a Ken-sha. * [[Shōwa]] 18 (1943), October 28 – The release of ''The Life of Matsu the Untamed'' popularizes the Kokura Gion Daiko nationally. * Shōwa 33 (1958), April 3 – Kokura Gion Daiko is designated an [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property]] by Fukuoka Prefecture. * Shōwa 62 (1987) – The current festival schedule is established. == Access == * A 4-minute (350 m) walk from [[Nishikokura Station]]. * Approximately 1.5 km from the [[Kitakyushu Urban Expressway]]’s {{ill|Ōtemachi Interchange|ja|大手町出入口}} and 1.1 km from {{ill|Katsuyama Interchange|ja|勝山出入口}}. == Nearby Attractions == * [[Kokura Castle]] * {{ill|Riverwalk Kitakyushu|ja|リバーウォーク北九州}} * [[Kitakyushu City Hall]] * {{ill|Katsuyama Park (Kitakyushu)|ja|勝山公園 (北九州市)}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Yasaka-jinja (Kitakyushu)}} * [http://www.yasaka-jinja.com/ Official Website of Yasaka Shrine] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{citation needed|Yasaka Shrine Kitakyushu}} [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines of Fukuoka]] [[Category:Shrines in Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Kokurakita Ward History]] [[Category:Kokurakita Ward Architecture]] [[Category:Yasaka Shrines]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yasaka Shrine (Kitakyushu)}}[[Category:Gion Shrines]] {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan02s3.jpg|300px]] | name = Yasaka Shrine | location = [[Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka]], [[Kitakyushu]], [[Kokurakita Ward]], {{ill|Jōnai|ja|城内 (北九州市)}} 2-2 | coordinates = {{Coord|33|53|07.68|N|130|52|25.39|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Yasaka Shrine}} | Deities = North Hall:
{{ill|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|櫛名田比売命}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐能袁命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto|ja|天之菩卑命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto|ja|天之忍穂耳命}}, {{ill|Amatsuhikone-no-Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命|1=Amatsuhikone}}, {{ill|Ikutsuhikone-no-Mikoto|ja|活津日子根命}}, {{ill|Kumano-Kusubi-no-Mikoto|ja|熊野久須比命}}, {{ill|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|ja|市寸島比売命}}, {{ill|Takiribime-no-Mikoto|ja|多紀理比売命}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto|ja|多岐津比売命}}
South Hall:
{{ill|Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|ja|大国主}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto|ja|少比古那命}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命}}, {{ill|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|櫛名田比売命}} | type = [[Ken-sha]] (as of May 15, [[Meiji]] 6) | Established = By {{ill|Hosokawa Tadaoki|ja|細川忠興}} in [[Genna (era)|Genna]] 3 (1617), February | Main Hall = Nagare-zukuri style | Festival = Gion Festival ([[Kokura Gion Daiko]]) on the third Saturday and Sunday of July | Rituals = }} [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan03s3.jpg|thumb|Haiden (Worship Hall)]] [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan08s3.jpg|thumb|Main Hall]] [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan09s3.jpg|thumb|East Gate]] '''Kokura Gion Yasaka Shrine''' (こくらぎおんやさかじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in {{ill|Jōnai|ja|城内 (北九州市)}}, [[Kokurakita Ward]], [[Kitakyushu]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]]. It enshrines deities as listed in the template above. Its previous ranking under the [[Modern System of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] was as a [[Ken-sha]] (Prefectural Shrine). == Overview == Historically called “Kokura no Gion-san,” the shrine is known for the {{ill|Kokura Gion Taiko|ja|小倉祇園太鼓}} festival depicted in the film adaptations of ''{{ill|The Life of Matsu the Untamed|ja|無法松の一生}}'' featuring actors like {{ill|Bando Tsumasaburō|ja|阪東妻三郎}} (1943 version) and {{ill|Mifune Toshirō|ja|三船敏郎}} (1958 version). The festival, first celebrated in [[Genna (era)|Genna]] 4 (1618), has over 390 years of history and now takes place annually on the third Saturday and Sunday of July. The shrine attracts the largest number of visitors in Kitakyushu and the surrounding areas. Initially called {{ill|Gion-sha|ja|祇園社}}, the shrine’s name was changed to Yasaka Shrine during the [[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto-Buddhism Separation Order]] of [[Meiji]] 4 (1868), which resulted in the broader {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}} movement. The shrine is currently an independent entity, unaffiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. == Timeline == * {{ill|Jōgan (era)|ja|貞観}} 12 (870), April – To combat an epidemic, the {{ill|Gion-sha|ja|祇園社}} from Yasaka in [[Yamashiro Province]] was enshrined at Nagao (near present-day {{ill|Nagao, Kokuraminami|ja|長尾 (小倉南区)}}), becoming the regional guardian of {{ill|Kiku District|ja|企救郡}}. * [[Keichō]] 7 (1602) – {{ill|Hosokawa Tadaoki|ja|細川忠興}} begins constructing [[Kokura Castle]]. * [[Genna (era)|Genna]] 3 (1617) – The shrine is moved to its current location in Kokura Castle’s northern grounds as the clan guardian shrine. The southern and northern halls house deities transferred from Nagao and Sambommatsu (present-day {{ill|Tanga Market|ja|旦過市場}}), respectively. * Genna 4 (1618) – Inspired by the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|ja|祇園祭}} in Kyoto, Hosokawa introduces the Kokura Gion Festival, initially featuring decorated floats. * [[Meiji]] 6 (1873), May 15 – Designated a Ken-sha. * [[Shōwa]] 18 (1943), October 28 – The release of ''The Life of Matsu the Untamed'' popularizes the Kokura Gion Daiko nationally. * Shōwa 33 (1958), April 3 – Kokura Gion Daiko is designated an [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property]] by Fukuoka Prefecture. * Shōwa 62 (1987) – The current festival schedule is established. == Access == * A 4-minute (350 m) walk from [[Nishikokura Station]]. * Approximately 1.5 km from the [[Kitakyushu Urban Expressway]]’s {{ill|Ōtemachi Interchange|ja|大手町出入口}} and 1.1 km from {{ill|Katsuyama Interchange|ja|勝山出入口}}. == Nearby Attractions == * [[Kokura Castle]] * {{ill|Riverwalk Kitakyushu|ja|リバーウォーク北九州}} * [[Kitakyushu City Hall]] * {{ill|Katsuyama Park (Kitakyushu)|ja|勝山公園 (北九州市)}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Yasaka-jinja (Kitakyushu)}} * [http://www.yasaka-jinja.com/ Official Website of Yasaka Shrine] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{citation needed|Yasaka Shrine Kitakyushu}} [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines of Fukuoka]] [[Category:Shrines in The release of ''The Life of Matsu the Untamed'' popularizes the Kokura Gion Daiko nationally. * Shōwa 33 (1958), April 3 – Kokura Gion Daiko is designated an [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property]] by Fukuoka Prefecture. * Shōwa 62 (1987) – The current festival schedule is established. == Access == * A 4-minute (350 m) walk from [[Nishikokura Station]]. * Approximately 1.5 km from the [[Kitakyushu Urban Expressway]]’s {{ill|Ōtemachi Interchange|ja|大手町出入口}} and 1.1 km from {{ill|Katsuyama Interchange|ja|勝山出入口}}. == Nearby Attractions == * [[Kokura Castle]] * {{ill|Riverwalk {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan02s3.jpg|300px]] | name = Yasaka Shrine | location = [[Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka]], [[Kitakyushu]], [[Kokurakita Ward]], {{ill|Jōnai|ja|城内 (北九州市)}} 2-2 | coordinates = {{Coord|33|53|07.68|N|130|52|25.39|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Yasaka Shrine}} | Deities = North Hall:
{{ill|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|櫛名田比売命}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐能袁命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto|ja|天之菩卑命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto|ja|天之忍穂耳命}}, {{ill|Amatsuhikone-no-Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命|1=Amatsuhikone}}, {{ill|Ikutsuhikone-no-Mikoto|ja|活津日子根命}}, {{ill|Kumano-Kusubi-no-Mikoto|ja|熊野久須比命}}, {{ill|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|ja|市寸島比売命}}, {{ill|Takiribime-no-Mikoto|ja|多紀理比売命}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto|ja|多岐津比売命}}
South Hall:
{{ill|Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|ja|大国主}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto|ja|少比古那命}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命}}, {{ill|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|櫛名田比売命}} | type = [[Ken-sha]] (as of May 15, [[Meiji]] 6) | Established = By {{ill|Hosokawa Tadaoki|ja|細川忠興}} in [[Genna (era)|Genna]] 3 (1617), February | Main Hall = Nagare-zukuri style | Festival = Gion Festival ([[Kokura Gion Daiko]]) on the third Saturday and Sunday of July | Rituals = }} [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan03s3.jpg|thumb|Haiden (Worship Hall)]] [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan08s3.jpg|thumb|Main Hall]] [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan09s3.jpg|thumb|East Gate]] '''Kokura Gion Yasaka Shrine''' (こくらぎおんやさかじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in {{ill|Jōnai|ja|城内 (北九州市)}}, [[Kokurakita Ward]], [[Kitakyushu]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]]. It enshrines deities as listed in the template above. Its previous ranking under the [[Modern System of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] was as a [[Ken-sha]] (Prefectural Shrine). == Overview == Historically called “Kokura no Gion-san,” the shrine is known for the {{ill|Kokura Gion Taiko|ja|小倉祇園太鼓}} festival depicted in the film adaptations of ''{{ill|The Life of Matsu the Untamed|ja|無法松の一生}}'' featuring actors like {{ill|Bando Tsumasaburō|ja|阪東妻三郎}} (1943 version) and {{ill|Mifune Toshirō|ja|三船敏郎}} (1958 version). The festival, first celebrated in [[Genna (era)|Genna]] 4 (1618), has over 390 years of history and now takes place annually on the third Saturday and Sunday of July. The shrine attracts the largest number of visitors in Kitakyushu and the surrounding areas. Initially called {{ill|Gion-sha|ja|祇園社}}, the shrine’s name was changed to Yasaka Shrine during the [[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto-Buddhism Separation Order]] of [[Meiji]] 4 (1868), which resulted in the broader {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}} movement. The shrine is currently an independent entity, unaffiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. == Timeline == * {{ill|Jōgan (era)|ja|貞観}} 12 (870), April – To combat an epidemic, the {{ill|Gion-sha|ja|祇園社}} from Yasaka in [[Yamashiro Province]] was enshrined at Nagao (near present-day {{ill|Nagao, Kokuraminami|ja|長尾 (小倉南区)}}), becoming the regional guardian of {{ill|Kiku District|ja|企救郡}}. * [[Keichō]] 7 (1602) – {{ill|Hosokawa Tadaoki|ja|細川忠興}} begins constructing [[Kokura Castle]]. * [[Genna (era)|Genna]] 3 (1617) – The shrine is moved to its current location in Kokura Castle’s northern grounds as the clan guardian shrine. The southern and northern halls house deities transferred from Nagao and Sambommatsu (present-day {{ill|Tanga Market|ja|旦過市場}}), respectively. * Genna 4 (1618) – Inspired by the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|ja|祇園祭}} in Kyoto, Hosokawa introduces the Kokura Gion Festival, initially featuring decorated floats. * [[Meiji]] 6 (1873), May 15 – Designated a Ken-sha. * [[Shōwa]] 18 (1943), October 28 – The release of ''The Life of Matsu the Untamed'' popularizes the Kokura Gion Daiko nationally. * Shōwa 33 (1958), April 3 – Kokura Gion Daiko is designated an [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property]] by Fukuoka Prefecture. * Shōwa 62 (1987) – The current festival schedule is established. == Access == * A 4-minute (350 m) walk from [[Nishikokura Station]]. * Approximately 1.5 km from the [[Kitakyushu Urban Expressway]]’s {{ill|Ōtemachi Interchange|ja|大手町出入口}} and 1.1 km from {{ill|Katsuyama Interchange|ja|勝山出入口}}. == Nearby Attractions == * [[Kokura Castle]] * {{ill|Riverwalk Kitakyushu|ja|リバーウォーク北九州}} * [[Kitakyushu City Hall]] * {{ill|Katsuyama Park (Kitakyushu)|ja|勝山公園 (北九州市)}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Yasaka-jinja (Kitakyushu)}} * [http://www.yasaka-jinja.com/ Official Website of Yasaka Shrine] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{citation needed|Yasaka Shrine Kitakyushu}} [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines of Fukuoka]] [[Category:Shrines in Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Kokurakita Ward History]] [[Category:Kokurakita Ward Architecture]] [[Category:Yasaka Shrines]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yasaka Shrine (Kitakyushu)}}[[Category:Gion Shrines]] The shrine is currently an independent entity, unaffiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. == Timeline == * {{ill|Jōgan (era)|ja|貞観}} 12 (870), April – To combat an epidemic, the {{ill|Gion-sha|ja|祇園社}} from Yasaka in [[Yamashiro Province]] was enshrined at Nagao (near present-day {{ill|Nagao, Kokuraminami|ja|長尾 (小倉南区)}}), becoming the regional guardian of {{ill|Kiku District|ja|企救郡}}. * [[Keichō]] 7 (1602) – {{ill|Hosokawa Tadaoki|ja|細川忠興}} begins constructing [[Kokura Castle]]. * [[Genna (era)|Genna]] 3 (1617) – The shrine is moved to its current location in Kokura Castle’s northern grounds as the clan guardian shrine. The southern and northern halls house deities transferred from Nagao and Sambommatsu (present-day {{ill|Tanga Market|ja|旦過市場}}), respectively. * Genna 4 (1618) – Inspired by the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|ja|祇園祭}} in Kyoto, Hosokawa introduces the Kokura Gion Festival, initially featuring decorated floats. * [[Meiji]] 6 (1873), May 15 – Designated a Ken-sha. * [[Shōwa]] 18 (1943), October 28 – The release of ''The Life of Matsu the Untamed'' popularizes the Kokura Gion Daiko nationally. * Shōwa 33 (1958), April 3 – Kokura Gion Daiko is designated an [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property]] by Fukuoka Prefecture. * Shōwa 62 (1987) – The current festival schedule is established. == Access == * A 4-minute (350 m) walk from [[Nishikokura Station]]. * Approximately 1.5 km from the [[Kitakyushu Urban Expressway]]’s {{ill|Ōtemachi Interchange|ja|大手町出入口}} and 1.1 km from {{ill|Katsuyama Interchange|ja|勝山出入口}}. == Nearby Attractions == * [[Kokura Castle]] * {{ill|Riverwalk Kitakyushu|ja|リバーウォーク北九州}} * [[Kitakyushu City Hall]] * {{ill|Katsuyama Park (Kitakyushu)|ja|勝山公園 (北九州市)}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Yasaka-jinja (Kitakyushu)}} * [http://www.yasaka-jinja.com/ {{ill|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|櫛名田比売命}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐能袁命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto|ja|天之菩卑命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto|ja|天之忍穂耳命}}, {{ill|Amatsuhikone-no-Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命|1=Amatsuhikone}}, {{ill|Ikutsuhikone-no-Mikoto|ja|活津日子根命}}, {{ill|Kumano-Kusubi-no-Mikoto|ja|熊野久須比命}}, {{ill|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|ja|市寸島比売命}}, {{ill|Takiribime-no-Mikoto|ja|多紀理比売命}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto|ja|多岐津比売命}}

{{ill|Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|ja|大国主}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto|ja|少比古那命}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命}}, {{ill|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|櫛名田比売命}} | type = [[Ken-sha]] (as of May 15, [[Meiji]] 6) | Established = By {{ill|Hosokawa Tadaoki|ja|細川忠興}} in [[Genna (era)|Genna]] 3 (1617), February | Main Hall = Nagare-zukuri style | Festival = Gion Festival ([[Kokura Gion Daiko]]) on the third Saturday and Sunday of July | Rituals = }} [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan03s3.jpg|thumb|Haiden (Worship Hall)]] [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan08s3.jpg|thumb|Main Hall]] [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan09s3.jpg|thumb|East Gate]] '''Kokura Gion Yasaka Shrine''' (こくらぎおんやさかじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in {{ill|Jōnai|ja|城内 (北九州市)}}, [[Kokurakita Ward]], [[Kitakyushu]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]]. It enshrines deities as listed in the template above. Its previous ranking under the [[Modern System of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] was as a [[Ken-sha]] (Prefectural Shrine). == Overview == Historically called “Kokura no Gion-san,” the shrine is known for the {{ill|Kokura Gion Taiko|ja|小倉祇園太鼓}} festival depicted in the film adaptations of ''{{ill|The Life of Matsu the Untamed|ja|無法松の一生}}'' featuring actors like {{ill|Bando Tsumasaburō|ja|阪東妻三郎}} (1943 version) and {{ill|Mifune Toshirō|ja|三船敏郎}} (1958 version). The festival, first celebrated in [[Genna (era)|Genna]] 4 (1618), has over 390 years of history and now takes place annually on the third Saturday and Sunday of July. The shrine attracts the largest number of visitors in Kitakyushu and the surrounding areas. Initially called {{ill|Gion-sha|ja|祇園社}}, the shrine’s name was changed to Yasaka Shrine during the [[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto-Buddhism Separation Order]] of [[Meiji]] 4 (1868), which resulted in the broader {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}} movement. The southern and northern halls house deities transferred from Nagao and Sambommatsu (present-day {{ill|Tanga Market|ja|旦過市場}}), respectively. * Genna 4 (1618) – Inspired by the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|ja|祇園祭}} in Kyoto, Hosokawa introduces the Kokura Gion Festival, initially featuring decorated floats. * [[Meiji]] 6 (1873), May 15 – Designated a Ken-sha. * [[Shōwa]] 18 (1943), October 28 – The release of ''The Life of Matsu the Untamed'' popularizes the Kokura Gion Daiko nationally. * Shōwa 33 (1958), April 3 – Kokura Gion Daiko is designated an [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property]] by Fukuoka Prefecture. * Shōwa 62 (1987) – The current festival schedule is established. == Access == * A 4-minute (350 m) walk from [[Nishikokura Station]]. * Approximately 1.5 km from the [[Kitakyushu Urban Expressway]]’s {{ill|Ōtemachi Interchange|ja|大手町出入口}} and 1.1 km from {{ill|Katsuyama Interchange|ja|勝山出入口}}. == Nearby Attractions == * [[Kokura Castle]] * {{ill|Riverwalk Its previous ranking under the [[Modern System of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] was as a [[Ken-sha]] (Prefectural Shrine). == Overview == Historically called “Kokura no Gion-san,” the shrine is known for the {{ill|Kokura Gion Taiko|ja|小倉祇園太鼓}} festival depicted in the film adaptations of ''{{ill|The Life of Matsu the Untamed|ja|無法松の一生}}'' featuring actors like {{ill|Bando Tsumasaburō|ja|阪東妻三郎}} (1943 version) and {{ill|Mifune Toshirō|ja|三船敏郎}} (1958 version). The festival, first celebrated in [[Genna (era)|Genna]] 4 (1618), has over 390 years of history and now takes place annually on the third Saturday and Sunday of July. The shrine attracts the largest number of visitors in Kitakyushu and the surrounding areas. Initially called {{ill|Gion-sha|ja|祇園社}}, the shrine’s name was changed to Yasaka Shrine during the [[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto-Buddhism Separation Order]] of [[Meiji]] 4 (1868), which resulted in the broader {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}} movement. The shrine is currently an independent entity, unaffiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. == Timeline == * {{ill|Jōgan (era)|ja|貞観}} 12 (870), April – To combat an epidemic, the {{ill|Gion-sha|ja|祇園社}} from Yasaka in [[Yamashiro Province]] was enshrined at Nagao (near present-day {{ill|Nagao, Kokuraminami|ja|長尾 (小倉南区)}}), becoming the regional guardian of {{ill|Kiku District|ja|企救郡}}. * [[Keichō]] 7 (1602) – {{ill|Hosokawa Tadaoki|ja|細川忠興}} begins constructing [[Kokura Castle]]. * [[Genna (era)|Genna]] 3 (1617) – The shrine is moved to its current location in Kokura Castle’s northern grounds as the clan guardian shrine. The southern and northern halls house deities transferred from Nagao and Sambommatsu (present-day {{ill|Tanga Market|ja|旦過市場}}), respectively. * Genna 4 (1618) – Inspired by the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|ja|祇園祭}} in Kyoto, Hosokawa introduces the Kokura Gion Festival, initially featuring decorated floats. * [[Meiji]] 6 (1873), May 15 – Designated a Ken-sha. * [[Shōwa]] 18 (1943), October 28 – The release of ''The Life of Matsu the Untamed'' popularizes the Kokura Gion Daiko nationally. * Shōwa 33 (1958), April 3 – Kokura Gion Daiko is designated an [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property]] by Fukuoka Prefecture. * Shōwa 62 (1987) – The current festival schedule is established. == Access == * A 4-minute (350 m) walk from [[Nishikokura Station]]. * Approximately 1.5 km from the [[Kitakyushu Urban Expressway]]’s {{ill|Ōtemachi Interchange|ja|大手町出入口}} and 1.1 km from {{ill|Katsuyama Interchange|ja|勝山出入口}}. == Nearby Attractions == * [[Kokura Castle]] * {{ill|Riverwalk Kitakyushu|ja|リバーウォーク北九州}} * [[Kitakyushu City Hall]] * {{ill|Katsuyama Park (Kitakyushu)|ja|勝山公園 (北九州市)}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Yasaka-jinja (Kitakyushu)}} * [http://www.yasaka-jinja.com/ Official Website of Yasaka Shrine] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{citation needed|Yasaka Shrine Kitakyushu}} [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines of Fukuoka]] [[Category:Shrines in Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Kokurakita Ward History]] [[Category:Kokurakita Ward Architecture]] [[Category:Yasaka Shrines]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yasaka Shrine (Kitakyushu)}}[[Category:Gion Shrines]] {{ill|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|櫛名田比売命}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐能袁命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto|ja|天之菩卑命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto|ja|天之忍穂耳命}}, {{ill|Amatsuhikone-no-Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命|1=Amatsuhikone}}, {{ill|Ikutsuhikone-no-Mikoto|ja|活津日子根命}}, {{ill|Kumano-Kusubi-no-Mikoto|ja|熊野久須比命}}, {{ill|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|ja|市寸島比売命}}, {{ill|Takiribime-no-Mikoto|ja|多紀理比売命}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto|ja|多岐津比売命}}
South Hall:
{{ill|Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|ja|大国主}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto|ja|少比古那命}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命}}, {{ill|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|櫛名田比売命}} | type = [[Ken-sha]] (as of May 15, [[Meiji]] 6) | Established = By {{ill|Hosokawa Tadaoki|ja|細川忠興}} in [[Genna (era)|Genna]] 3 (1617), February | Main Hall = Nagare-zukuri style | Festival = Gion Festival ([[Kokura Gion Daiko]]) on the third Saturday and Sunday of July | Rituals = }} [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan03s3.jpg|thumb|Haiden (Worship Hall)]] [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan08s3.jpg|thumb|Main Hall]] [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan09s3.jpg|thumb|East Gate]] '''Kokura Gion Yasaka Shrine''' (こくらぎおんやさかじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in {{ill|Jōnai|ja|城内 (北九州市)}}, [[Kokurakita Ward]], [[Kitakyushu]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]]. It enshrines deities as listed in the template above. Its previous ranking under the [[Modern System of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] was as a [[Ken-sha]] (Prefectural Shrine). == Overview == Historically called “Kokura no Gion-san,” the shrine is known for the {{ill|Kokura Gion Taiko|ja|小倉祇園太鼓}} festival depicted in the film adaptations of ''{{ill|The Life of Matsu the Untamed|ja|無法松の一生}}'' featuring actors like {{ill|Bando Tsumasaburō|ja|阪東妻三郎}} (1943 version) and {{ill|Mifune Toshirō|ja|三船敏郎}} (1958 version). The festival, first celebrated in [[Genna (era)|Genna]] 4 (1618), has over 390 years of history and now takes place annually on the third Saturday and Sunday of July. The shrine attracts the largest number of visitors in Kitakyushu and the surrounding areas. Initially called {{ill|Gion-sha|ja|祇園社}}, the shrine’s name was changed to Yasaka Shrine during the [[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto-Buddhism Separation Order]] of [[Meiji]] 4 (1868), which resulted in the broader {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}} movement. The shrine is currently an independent entity, unaffiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. == Timeline == * {{ill|Jōgan (era)|ja|貞観}} 12 (870), April – To combat an epidemic, the {{ill|Gion-sha|ja|祇園社}} from Yasaka in [[Yamashiro Province]] was enshrined at Nagao (near present-day {{ill|Nagao, Kokuraminami|ja|長尾 (小倉南区)}}), becoming the regional guardian of {{ill|Kiku District|ja|企救郡}}. * [[Keichō]] 7 (1602) – {{ill|Hosokawa Tadaoki|ja|細川忠興}} begins constructing [[Kokura Castle]]. * [[Genna (era)|Genna]] 3 (1617) – The shrine is moved to its current location in Kokura Castle’s northern grounds as the clan guardian shrine. The southern and northern halls house deities transferred from Nagao and Sambommatsu (present-day {{ill|Tanga Market|ja|旦過市場}}), respectively. * Genna 4 (1618) – Inspired by the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|ja|祇園祭}} in Kyoto, Hosokawa introduces the Kokura Gion Festival, initially featuring decorated floats. * [[Meiji]] 6 (1873), May 15 – Designated a Ken-sha. * [[Shōwa]] 18 (1943), October 28 – The release of ''The Life of Matsu the Untamed'' popularizes the Kokura Gion Daiko nationally. * Shōwa 33 (1958), April 3 – Kokura Gion Daiko is designated an [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property]] by Fukuoka Prefecture. * Shōwa 62 (1987) – The current festival schedule is established. == Access == * A 4-minute (350 m) walk from [[Nishikokura Station]]. * Approximately 1.5 km from the [[Kitakyushu Urban Expressway]]’s {{ill|Ōtemachi Interchange|ja|大手町出入口}} and 1.1 km from {{ill|Katsuyama Interchange|ja|勝山出入口}}. == Nearby Attractions == * [[Kokura Castle]] * {{ill|Riverwalk Kitakyushu|ja|リバーウォーク北九州}} * [[Kitakyushu City Hall]] * {{ill|Katsuyama Park (Kitakyushu)|ja|勝山公園 (北九州市)}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Yasaka-jinja (Kitakyushu)}} * [http://www.yasaka-jinja.com/ {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan02s3.jpg|300px]] | name = Yasaka Shrine | location = [[Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka]], [[Kitakyushu]], [[Kokurakita Ward]], {{ill|Jōnai|ja|城内 (北九州市)}} 2-2 | coordinates = {{Coord|33|53|07.68|N|130|52|25.39|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Yasaka Shrine}} | Deities = North Hall:
{{ill|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|櫛名田比売命}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐能袁命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto|ja|天之菩卑命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto|ja|天之忍穂耳命}}, {{ill|Amatsuhikone-no-Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命|1=Amatsuhikone}}, {{ill|Ikutsuhikone-no-Mikoto|ja|活津日子根命}}, {{ill|Kumano-Kusubi-no-Mikoto|ja|熊野久須比命}}, {{ill|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|ja|市寸島比売命}}, {{ill|Takiribime-no-Mikoto|ja|多紀理比売命}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto|ja|多岐津比売命}}
South Hall:
{{ill|Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|ja|大国主}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto|ja|少比古那命}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命}}, {{ill|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|櫛名田比売命}} | type = [[Ken-sha]] (as of May 15, [[Meiji]] 6) | Established = By {{ill|Hosokawa Tadaoki|ja|細川忠興}} in [[Genna (era)|Genna]] 3 (1617), February | Main Hall = Nagare-zukuri style | Festival = Gion Festival ([[Kokura Gion Daiko]]) on the third Saturday and Sunday of July | Rituals = }} [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan03s3.jpg|thumb|Haiden (Worship Hall)]] [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan08s3.jpg|thumb|Main Hall]] [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan09s3.jpg|thumb|East Gate]] '''Kokura Gion Yasaka Shrine''' (こくらぎおんやさかじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in {{ill|Jōnai|ja|城内 (北九州市)}}, [[Kokurakita Ward]], [[Kitakyushu]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]]. It enshrines deities as listed in the template above. Its previous ranking under the [[Modern System of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] was as a [[Ken-sha]] (Prefectural Shrine). == Overview == Historically called “Kokura no Gion-san,” the shrine is known for the {{ill|Kokura Gion Taiko|ja|小倉祇園太鼓}} festival depicted in the film adaptations of ''{{ill|The Life of Matsu the Untamed|ja|無法松の一生}}'' featuring actors like {{ill|Bando Tsumasaburō|ja|阪東妻三郎}} (1943 version) and {{ill|Mifune Toshirō|ja|三船敏郎}} (1958 version). The festival, first celebrated in [[Genna (era)|Genna]] 4 (1618), has over 390 years of history and now takes place annually on the third Saturday and Sunday of July. The shrine attracts the largest number of visitors in Kitakyushu and the surrounding areas. Initially called {{ill|Gion-sha|ja|祇園社}}, the shrine’s name was changed to Yasaka Shrine during the [[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto-Buddhism Separation Order]] of [[Meiji]] 4 (1868), which resulted in the broader {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}} movement. The shrine is currently an independent entity, unaffiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. == Timeline == * {{ill|Jōgan (era)|ja|貞観}} 12 (870), April – To combat an epidemic, the {{ill|Gion-sha|ja|祇園社}} from Yasaka in [[Yamashiro Province]] was enshrined at Nagao (near present-day {{ill|Nagao, Kokuraminami|ja|長尾 (小倉南区)}}), becoming the regional guardian of {{ill|Kiku District|ja|企救郡}}. * [[Keichō]] 7 (1602) – {{ill|Hosokawa Tadaoki|ja|細川忠興}} begins constructing [[Kokura Castle]]. * [[Genna (era)|Genna]] 3 (1617) – The shrine is moved to its current location in Kokura Castle’s northern grounds as the clan guardian shrine. The southern and northern halls house deities transferred from Nagao and Sambommatsu (present-day {{ill|Tanga Market|ja|旦過市場}}), respectively. * Genna 4 (1618) – Inspired by the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|ja|祇園祭}} in Kyoto, Hosokawa introduces the Kokura Gion Festival, initially featuring decorated floats. * [[Meiji]] 6 (1873), May 15 – Designated a Ken-sha. * [[Shōwa]] 18 (1943), October 28 – The release of ''The Life of Matsu the Untamed'' popularizes the Kokura Gion Daiko nationally. * Shōwa 33 (1958), April 3 – Kokura Gion Daiko is designated an [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property]] by Fukuoka Prefecture. * Shōwa 62 (1987) – The current festival schedule is established. == Access == * A 4-minute (350 m) walk from [[Nishikokura Station]]. * Approximately 1.5 km from the [[Kitakyushu Urban Expressway]]’s {{ill|Ōtemachi Interchange|ja|大手町出入口}} and 1.1 km from {{ill|Katsuyama Interchange|ja|勝山出入口}}. == Nearby Attractions == * [[Kokura Castle]] * {{ill|Riverwalk Kitakyushu|ja|リバーウォーク北九州}} * [[Kitakyushu City Hall]] * {{ill|Katsuyama Park (Kitakyushu)|ja|勝山公園 (北九州市)}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Yasaka-jinja (Kitakyushu)}} * [http://www.yasaka-jinja.com/ Official Website of Yasaka Shrine] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{citation needed|Yasaka Shrine Kitakyushu}} [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines of Fukuoka]] [[Category:Shrines in Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Kokurakita Ward History]] [[Category:Kokurakita Ward Architecture]] [[Category:Yasaka Shrines]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yasaka Shrine (Kitakyushu)}}[[Category:Gion Shrines]] {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan02s3.jpg|300px]] | name = Yasaka Shrine | location = [[Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka]], [[Kitakyushu]], [[Kokurakita Ward]], {{ill|Jōnai|ja|城内 (北九州市)}} 2-2 | coordinates = {{Coord|33|53|07.68|N|130|52|25.39|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Yasaka Shrine}} | Deities = North Hall:
{{ill|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|櫛名田比売命}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐能袁命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto|ja|天之菩卑命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto|ja|天之忍穂耳命}}, {{ill|Amatsuhikone-no-Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命|1=Amatsuhikone}}, {{ill|Ikutsuhikone-no-Mikoto|ja|活津日子根命}}, {{ill|Kumano-Kusubi-no-Mikoto|ja|熊野久須比命}}, {{ill|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|ja|市寸島比売命}}, {{ill|Takiribime-no-Mikoto|ja|多紀理比売命}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto|ja|多岐津比売命}}
South Hall:
{{ill|Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|ja|大国主}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto|ja|少比古那命}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命}}, {{ill|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|櫛名田比売命}} | type = [[Ken-sha]] (as of May 15, [[Meiji]] 6) | Established = By {{ill|Hosokawa Tadaoki|ja|細川忠興}} in [[Genna (era)|Genna]] 3 (1617), February | Main Hall = Nagare-zukuri style | Festival = Gion Festival ([[Kokura Gion Daiko]]) on the third Saturday and Sunday of July | Rituals = }} [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan03s3.jpg|thumb|Haiden (Worship Hall)]] [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan08s3.jpg|thumb|Main Hall]] [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan09s3.jpg|thumb|East Gate]] '''Kokura Gion Yasaka Shrine''' (こくらぎおんやさかじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in {{ill|Jōnai|ja|城内 (北九州市)}}, [[Kokurakita Ward]], [[Kitakyushu]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]]. It enshrines deities as listed in the template above. Its previous ranking under the [[Modern System of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] was as a [[Ken-sha]] (Prefectural Shrine). == Overview == Historically called “Kokura no Gion-san,” the shrine is known for the {{ill|Kokura Gion Taiko|ja|小倉祇園太鼓}} festival depicted in the film adaptations of ''{{ill|The Life of Matsu the Untamed|ja|無法松の一生}}'' featuring actors like {{ill|Bando Tsumasaburō|ja|阪東妻三郎}} (1943 version) and {{ill|Mifune Toshirō|ja|三船敏郎}} (1958 version). The festival, first celebrated in [[Genna (era)|Genna]] 4 (1618), has over 390 years of history and now takes place annually on the third Saturday and Sunday of July. The shrine attracts the largest number of visitors in Kitakyushu and the surrounding areas. Initially called {{ill|Gion-sha|ja|祇園社}}, the shrine’s name was changed to Yasaka Shrine during the [[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto-Buddhism Separation Order]] of [[Meiji]] 4 (1868), which resulted in the broader {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}} movement. The shrine is currently an independent entity, unaffiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. == Timeline == * {{ill|Jōgan (era)|ja|貞観}} 12 (870), April – To combat an epidemic, the {{ill|Gion-sha|ja|祇園社}} from Yasaka in [[Yamashiro Province]] was enshrined at Nagao (near present-day {{ill|Nagao, Kokuraminami|ja|長尾 (小倉南区)}}), becoming the regional guardian of {{ill|Kiku District|ja|企救郡}}. * [[Keichō]] 7 (1602) – {{ill|Hosokawa Tadaoki|ja|細川忠興}} begins constructing [[Kokura Castle]]. * [[Genna (era)|Genna]] 3 (1617) – The shrine is moved to its current location in Kokura Castle’s northern grounds as the clan guardian shrine. The southern and northern halls house deities transferred from Nagao and Sambommatsu (present-day {{ill|Tanga Market|ja|旦過市場}}), respectively. * Genna 4 (1618) – Inspired by the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|ja|祇園祭}} in Kyoto, Hosokawa introduces the Kokura Gion Festival, initially featuring decorated floats. * [[Meiji]] 6 (1873), May 15 – Designated a Ken-sha. * [[Shōwa]] 18 (1943), October 28 – The release of ''The Life of Matsu the Untamed'' popularizes the Kokura Gion Daiko nationally. * Shōwa 33 (1958), April 3 – Kokura Gion Daiko is designated an [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property]] by Fukuoka Prefecture. * Shōwa 62 (1987) – The current festival schedule is established. == Access == * A 4-minute (350 m) walk from [[Nishikokura Station]]. * Approximately 1.5 km from the [[Kitakyushu Urban Expressway]]’s {{ill|Ōtemachi Interchange|ja|大手町出入口}} and 1.1 km from {{ill|Katsuyama Interchange|ja|勝山出入口}}. == Nearby Attractions == * [[Kokura Castle]] * {{ill|Riverwalk Kitakyushu|ja|リバーウォーク北九州}} * [[Kitakyushu City Hall]] * {{ill|Katsuyama Park (Kitakyushu)|ja|勝山公園 (北九州市)}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Yasaka-jinja (Kitakyushu)}} * [http://www.yasaka-jinja.com/ Official Website of Yasaka Shrine] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{citation needed|Yasaka Shrine Kitakyushu}} [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines of Fukuoka]] [[Category:Shrines in {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan02s3.jpg|300px]] | name = Yasaka Shrine | location = [[Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka]], [[Kitakyushu]], [[Kokurakita Ward]], {{ill|Jōnai|ja|城内 (北九州市)}} 2-2 | coordinates = {{Coord|33|53|07.68|N|130|52|25.39|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Yasaka Shrine}} | Deities = North Hall:
{{ill|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|櫛名田比売命}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐能袁命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto|ja|天之菩卑命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto|ja|天之忍穂耳命}}, {{ill|Amatsuhikone-no-Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命|1=Amatsuhikone}}, {{ill|Ikutsuhikone-no-Mikoto|ja|活津日子根命}}, {{ill|Kumano-Kusubi-no-Mikoto|ja|熊野久須比命}}, {{ill|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|ja|市寸島比売命}}, {{ill|Takiribime-no-Mikoto|ja|多紀理比売命}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto|ja|多岐津比売命}}
South Hall:
{{ill|Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|ja|大国主}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto|ja|少比古那命}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命}}, {{ill|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|櫛名田比売命}} | type = [[Ken-sha]] (as of May 15, [[Meiji]] 6) | Established = By {{ill|Hosokawa Tadaoki|ja|細川忠興}} in [[Genna (era)|Genna]] 3 (1617), February | Main Hall = Nagare-zukuri style | Festival = Gion Festival ([[Kokura Gion Daiko]]) on the third Saturday and Sunday of July | Rituals = }} [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan03s3.jpg|thumb|Haiden (Worship Hall)]] [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan08s3.jpg|thumb|Main Hall]] [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan09s3.jpg|thumb|East Gate]] '''Kokura Gion Yasaka Shrine''' (こくらぎおんやさかじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in {{ill|Jōnai|ja|城内 (北九州市)}}, [[Kokurakita Ward]], [[Kitakyushu]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]]. It enshrines deities as listed in the template above. Its previous ranking under the [[Modern System of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] was as a [[Ken-sha]] (Prefectural Shrine). == Overview == Historically called “Kokura no Gion-san,” the shrine is known for the {{ill|Kokura Gion Taiko|ja|小倉祇園太鼓}} festival depicted in the film adaptations of ''{{ill|The Life of Matsu the Untamed|ja|無法松の一生}}'' featuring actors like {{ill|Bando Tsumasaburō|ja|阪東妻三郎}} (1943 version) and {{ill|Mifune Toshirō|ja|三船敏郎}} (1958 version). The festival, first celebrated in [[Genna (era)|Genna]] 4 (1618), has over 390 years of history and now takes place annually on the third Saturday and Sunday of July. The shrine attracts the largest number of visitors in Kitakyushu and the surrounding areas. Initially called {{ill|Gion-sha|ja|祇園社}}, the shrine’s name was changed to Yasaka Shrine during the [[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto-Buddhism Separation Order]] of [[Meiji]] 4 (1868), which resulted in the broader {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}} movement. The shrine is currently an independent entity, unaffiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. == Timeline == * {{ill|Jōgan (era)|ja|貞観}} 12 (870), April – To combat an epidemic, the {{ill|Gion-sha|ja|祇園社}} from Yasaka in [[Yamashiro Province]] was enshrined at Nagao (near present-day {{ill|Nagao, Kokuraminami|ja|長尾 (小倉南区)}}), becoming the regional guardian of {{ill|Kiku District|ja|企救郡}}. * [[Keichō]] 7 (1602) – {{ill|Hosokawa Tadaoki|ja|細川忠興}} begins constructing [[Kokura Castle]]. * [[Genna (era)|Genna]] 3 (1617) – The shrine is moved to its current location in Kokura Castle’s northern grounds as the clan guardian shrine. The southern and northern halls house deities transferred from Nagao and Sambommatsu (present-day {{ill|Tanga Market|ja|旦過市場}}), respectively. * Genna 4 (1618) – Inspired by the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|ja|祇園祭}} in Kyoto, Hosokawa introduces the Kokura Gion Festival, initially featuring decorated floats. * [[Meiji]] 6 (1873), May 15 – Designated a Ken-sha. * [[Shōwa]] 18 (1943), October 28 – The release of ''The Life of Matsu the Untamed'' popularizes the Kokura Gion Daiko nationally. * Shōwa 33 (1958), April 3 – Kokura Gion Daiko is designated an [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property]] by Fukuoka Prefecture. * Shōwa 62 (1987) – The current festival schedule is established. == Access == * A 4-minute (350 m) walk from [[Nishikokura Station]]. * Approximately 1.5 km from the [[Kitakyushu Urban Expressway]]’s {{ill|Ōtemachi Interchange|ja|大手町出入口}} and 1.1 km from {{ill|Katsuyama Interchange|ja|勝山出入口}}. == Nearby Attractions == * [[Kokura Castle]] * {{ill|Riverwalk Kitakyushu|ja|リバーウォーク北九州}} * [[Kitakyushu City Hall]] * {{ill|Katsuyama Park (Kitakyushu)|ja|勝山公園 (北九州市)}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Yasaka-jinja (Kitakyushu)}} * [http://www.yasaka-jinja.com/ Official Website of Yasaka Shrine] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{citation needed|Yasaka Shrine Kitakyushu}} [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines of Fukuoka]] [[Category:Shrines in {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan02s3.jpg|300px]] | name = Yasaka Shrine | location = [[Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka]], [[Kitakyushu]], [[Kokurakita Ward]], {{ill|Jōnai|ja|城内 (北九州市)}} 2-2 | coordinates = {{Coord|33|53|07.68|N|130|52|25.39|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Yasaka Shrine}} | Deities = North Hall:
{{ill|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|櫛名田比売命}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐能袁命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto|ja|天之菩卑命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto|ja|天之忍穂耳命}}, {{ill|Amatsuhikone-no-Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命|1=Amatsuhikone}}, {{ill|Ikutsuhikone-no-Mikoto|ja|活津日子根命}}, {{ill|Kumano-Kusubi-no-Mikoto|ja|熊野久須比命}}, {{ill|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|ja|市寸島比売命}}, {{ill|Takiribime-no-Mikoto|ja|多紀理比売命}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto|ja|多岐津比売命}}
South Hall:
{{ill|Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|ja|大国主}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto|ja|少比古那命}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命}}, {{ill|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|櫛名田比売命}} | type = [[Ken-sha]] (as of May 15, [[Meiji]] 6) | Established = By {{ill|Hosokawa Tadaoki|ja|細川忠興}} in [[Genna (era)|Genna]] 3 (1617), February | Main Hall = Nagare-zukuri style | Festival = Gion Festival ([[Kokura Gion Daiko]]) on the third Saturday and Sunday of July | Rituals = }} [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan03s3.jpg|thumb|Haiden (Worship Hall)]] [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan08s3.jpg|thumb|Main Hall]] [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan09s3.jpg|thumb|East Gate]] '''Kokura Gion Yasaka Shrine''' (こくらぎおんやさかじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in {{ill|Jōnai|ja|城内 (北九州市)}}, [[Kokurakita Ward]], [[Kitakyushu]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]]. It enshrines deities as listed in the template above. Its previous ranking under the [[Modern System of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] was as a [[Ken-sha]] (Prefectural Shrine). == Overview == Historically called “Kokura no Gion-san,” the shrine is known for the {{ill|Kokura Gion Taiko|ja|小倉祇園太鼓}} festival depicted in the film adaptations of ''{{ill|The Life of Matsu the Untamed|ja|無法松の一生}}'' featuring actors like {{ill|Bando Tsumasaburō|ja|阪東妻三郎}} (1943 version) and {{ill|Mifune Toshirō|ja|三船敏郎}} (1958 version). The festival, first celebrated in [[Genna (era)|Genna]] 4 (1618), has over 390 years of history and now takes place annually on the third Saturday and Sunday of July. The shrine attracts the largest number of visitors in Kitakyushu and the surrounding areas. Initially called {{ill|Gion-sha|ja|祇園社}}, the shrine’s name was changed to Yasaka Shrine during the [[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto-Buddhism Separation Order]] of [[Meiji]] 4 (1868), which resulted in the broader {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}} movement. The shrine is currently an independent entity, unaffiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. == Timeline == * {{ill|Jōgan (era)|ja|貞観}} 12 (870), April – To combat an epidemic, the {{ill|Gion-sha|ja|祇園社}} from Yasaka in [[Yamashiro Province]] was enshrined at Nagao (near present-day {{ill|Nagao, Kokuraminami|ja|長尾 (小倉南区)}}), becoming the regional guardian of {{ill|Kiku District|ja|企救郡}}. * [[Keichō]] 7 (1602) – {{ill|Hosokawa Tadaoki|ja|細川忠興}} begins constructing [[Kokura Castle]]. * [[Genna (era)|Genna]] 3 (1617) – The shrine is moved to its current location in Kokura Castle’s northern grounds as the clan guardian shrine. The southern and northern halls house deities transferred from Nagao and Sambommatsu (present-day {{ill|Tanga Market|ja|旦過市場}}), respectively. * Genna 4 (1618) – Inspired by the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|ja|祇園祭}} in Kyoto, Hosokawa introduces the Kokura Gion Festival, initially featuring decorated floats. * [[Meiji]] 6 (1873), May 15 – Designated a Ken-sha. * [[Shōwa]] 18 (1943), October 28 – The release of ''The Life of Matsu the Untamed'' popularizes the Kokura Gion Daiko nationally. * Shōwa 33 (1958), April 3 – Kokura Gion Daiko is designated an [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property]] by Fukuoka Prefecture. * Shōwa 62 (1987) – The current festival schedule is established. == Access == * A 4-minute (350 m) walk from [[Nishikokura Station]]. * Approximately 1.5 km from the [[Kitakyushu Urban Expressway]]’s {{ill|Ōtemachi Interchange|ja|大手町出入口}} and 1.1 km from {{ill|Katsuyama Interchange|ja|勝山出入口}}. == Nearby Attractions == * [[Kokura Castle]] * {{ill|Riverwalk Kitakyushu|ja|リバーウォーク北九州}} * [[Kitakyushu City Hall]] * {{ill|Katsuyama Park (Kitakyushu)|ja|勝山公園 (北九州市)}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Yasaka-jinja (Kitakyushu)}} * [http://www.yasaka-jinja.com/ Official Website of Yasaka Shrine] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{citation needed|Yasaka Shrine Kitakyushu}} [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines of Fukuoka]] [[Category:Shrines in {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan02s3.jpg|300px]] | name = Yasaka Shrine | location = [[Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka]], [[Kitakyushu]], [[Kokurakita Ward]], {{ill|Jōnai|ja|城内 (北九州市)}} 2-2 | coordinates = {{Coord|33|53|07.68|N|130|52|25.39|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Yasaka Shrine}} | Deities = North Hall:
{{ill|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|櫛名田比売命}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐能袁命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto|ja|天之菩卑命}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto|ja|天之忍穂耳命}}, {{ill|Amatsuhikone-no-Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命|1=Amatsuhikone}}, {{ill|Ikutsuhikone-no-Mikoto|ja|活津日子根命}}, {{ill|Kumano-Kusubi-no-Mikoto|ja|熊野久須比命}}, {{ill|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|ja|市寸島比売命}}, {{ill|Takiribime-no-Mikoto|ja|多紀理比売命}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto|ja|多岐津比売命}}
South Hall:
{{ill|Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|ja|大国主}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto|ja|少比古那命}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命}}, {{ill|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|櫛名田比売命}} | type = [[Ken-sha]] (as of May 15, [[Meiji]] 6) | Established = By {{ill|Hosokawa Tadaoki|ja|細川忠興}} in [[Genna (era)|Genna]] 3 (1617), February | Main Hall = Nagare-zukuri style | Festival = Gion Festival ([[Kokura Gion Daiko]]) on the third Saturday and Sunday of July | Rituals = }} [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan03s3.jpg|thumb|Haiden (Worship Hall)]] [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan08s3.jpg|thumb|Main Hall]] [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan09s3.jpg|thumb|East Gate]] '''Kokura Gion Yasaka Shrine''' (こくらぎおんやさかじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in {{ill|Jōnai|ja|城内 (北九州市)}}, [[Kokurakita Ward]], [[Kitakyushu]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]]. It enshrines deities as listed in the template above. Its previous ranking under the [[Modern System of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] was as a [[Ken-sha]] (Prefectural Shrine). == Overview == Historically called “Kokura no Gion-san,” the shrine is known for the {{ill|Kokura Gion Taiko|ja|小倉祇園太鼓}} festival depicted in the film adaptations of ''{{ill|The Life of Matsu the Untamed|ja|無法松の一生}}'' featuring actors like {{ill|Bando Tsumasaburō|ja|阪東妻三郎}} (1943 version) and {{ill|Mifune Toshirō|ja|三船敏郎}} (1958 version). The festival, first celebrated in [[Genna (era)|Genna]] 4 (1618), has over 390 years of history and now takes place annually on the third Saturday and Sunday of July. The shrine attracts the largest number of visitors in Kitakyushu and the surrounding areas. Initially called {{ill|Gion-sha|ja|祇園社}}, the shrine’s name was changed to Yasaka Shrine during the [[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto-Buddhism Separation Order]] of [[Meiji]] 4 (1868), which resulted in the broader {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈}} movement. The shrine is currently an independent entity, unaffiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. == Timeline == * {{ill|Jōgan (era)|ja|貞観}} 12 (870), April – To combat an epidemic, the {{ill|Gion-sha|ja|祇園社}} from Yasaka in [[Yamashiro Province]] was enshrined at Nagao (near present-day {{ill|Nagao, Kokuraminami|ja|長尾 (小倉南区)}}), becoming the regional guardian of {{ill|Kiku District|ja|企救郡}}. * [[Keichō]] 7 (1602) – {{ill|Hosokawa Tadaoki|ja|細川忠興}} begins constructing [[Kokura Castle]]. * [[Genna (era)|Genna]] 3 (1617) – The shrine is moved to its current location in Kokura Castle’s northern grounds as the clan guardian shrine. The southern and northern halls house deities transferred from Nagao and Sambommatsu (present-day {{ill|Tanga Market|ja|旦過市場}}), respectively. * Genna 4 (1618) – Inspired by the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|ja|祇園祭}} in Kyoto, Hosokawa introduces the Kokura Gion Festival, initially featuring decorated floats. * [[Meiji]] 6 (1873), May 15 – Designated a Ken-sha. * [[Shōwa]] 18 (1943), October 28 – The release of ''The Life of Matsu the Untamed'' popularizes the Kokura Gion Daiko nationally. * Shōwa 33 (1958), April 3 – Kokura Gion Daiko is designated an [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property]] by Fukuoka Prefecture. * Shōwa 62 (1987) – The current festival schedule is established. == Access == * A 4-minute (350 m) walk from [[Nishikokura Station]]. * Approximately 1.5 km from the [[Kitakyushu Urban Expressway]]’s {{ill|Ōtemachi Interchange|ja|大手町出入口}} and 1.1 km from {{ill|Katsuyama Interchange|ja|勝山出入口}}. == Nearby Attractions == * [[Kokura Castle]] * {{ill|Riverwalk Kitakyushu|ja|リバーウォーク北九州}} * [[Kitakyushu City Hall]] * {{ill|Katsuyama Park (Kitakyushu)|ja|勝山公園 (北九州市)}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Yasaka-jinja (Kitakyushu)}} * [http://www.yasaka-jinja.com/ Official Website of Yasaka Shrine] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{citation needed|Yasaka Shrine Kitakyushu}} [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines of Fukuoka]] [[Category:Shrines in Kitakyushu]] [[Category:Kokurakita Ward History]] [[Category:Kokurakita Ward Architecture]] [[Category:Yasaka Shrines]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Yasaka Shrine (Kitakyushu)}}[[Category:Gion Shrines]] The southern and northern halls house deities transferred from Nagao and Sambommatsu (present-day {{ill|Tanga Market|ja|旦過市場}}), respectively. * Genna 4 (1618) – Inspired by the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|ja|祇園祭}} in Kyoto, Hosokawa introduces the Kokura Gion Festival, initially featuring decorated floats. * [[Meiji]] 6 (1873), May 15 – Designated a Ken-sha. * [[Shōwa]] 18 (1943), October 28 – The release of ''The Life of Matsu the Untamed'' popularizes the Kokura Gion Daiko nationally. * Shōwa 33 (1958), April 3 – Kokura Gion Daiko is designated an [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property]] by Fukuoka Prefecture. * Shōwa 62 (1987) – The current festival schedule is established. == Access == * A 4-minute (350 m) walk from [[Nishikokura Station]]. * Approximately 1.5 km from the {{ill|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|櫛名田比売命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐能袁命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Hohi-no-Mikoto|ja|天之菩卑命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ame-no-Oshihomimi-no-Mikoto|ja|天之忍穂耳命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Amatsuhikone-no-Mikoto|ja|天津日子根命|1=Amatsuhikone|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ikutsuhikone-no-Mikoto|ja|活津日子根命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kumano-Kusubi-no-Mikoto|ja|熊野久須比命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ichikishimahime-no-Mikoto|ja|市寸島比売命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Takiribime-no-Mikoto|ja|多紀理比売命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Tagitsuhime-no-Mikoto|ja|多岐津比売命|12=draft}}
South Hall:
{{ill|Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|ja|大国主|12=draft}}, {{ill|Sukunabikona-no-Mikoto|ja|少比古那命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|ja|須佐之男命|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|ja|櫛名田比売命|12=draft}} | type = [[Ken-sha]] (as of May 15, [[Meiji]] 6) | Established = By {{ill|Hosokawa Tadaoki|ja|細川忠興|12=draft}} in [[Genna (era)|Genna]] 3 (1617), February | Main Hall = Nagare-zukuri style | Festival = Gion Festival ([[Kokura Gion Daiko]]) on the third Saturday and Sunday of July | Rituals = }} [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan03s3.jpg|thumb|Haiden (Worship Hall)]] [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan08s3.jpg|thumb|Main Hall]] [[File:140721 Yasaka-jinja Kitakyushu Japan09s3.jpg|thumb|East Gate]] '''Kokura Gion Yasaka Shrine''' (こくらぎおんやさかじんじゃ) is a [[Shinto]] shrine located in {{ill|Jōnai|ja|城内 (北九州市)|12=draft}}, [[Kokurakita Ward]], [[Kitakyushu]], [[Fukuoka Prefecture]]. It enshrines deities as listed in the template above. Its previous ranking under the [[Modern System of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] was as a [[Ken-sha]] (Prefectural Shrine). == Overview == Historically called “Kokura no Gion-san,” the shrine is known for the {{ill|Kokura Gion Taiko|ja|小倉祇園太鼓|12=draft}} festival depicted in the film adaptations of ''{{ill|The Life of Matsu the Untamed|ja|無法松の一生|12=draft}}'' featuring actors like {{ill|Bando Tsumasaburō|ja|阪東妻三郎|12=draft}} (1943 version) and {{ill|Mifune Toshirō|ja|三船敏郎|12=draft}} (1958 version). The festival, first celebrated in [[Genna (era)|Genna]] 4 (1618), has over 390 years of history and now takes place annually on the third Saturday and Sunday of July. The shrine attracts the largest number of visitors in Kitakyushu and the surrounding areas. Initially called {{ill|Gion-sha|ja|祇園社|12=draft}}, the shrine’s name was changed to Yasaka Shrine during the [[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto-Buddhism Separation Order]] of [[Meiji]] 4 (1868), which resulted in the broader {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|ja|廃仏毀釈|12=draft}} movement. The shrine is currently an independent entity, unaffiliated with the {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁|12=draft}}. == Timeline == * {{ill|Jōgan (era)|ja|貞観|12=draft}} 12 (870), April – To combat an epidemic, the {{ill|Gion-sha|ja|祇園社|12=draft}} from Yasaka in [[Yamashiro Province]] was enshrined at Nagao (near present-day {{ill|Nagao, Kokuraminami|ja|長尾 (小倉南区)|12=draft}}), becoming the regional guardian of {{ill|Kiku District|ja|企救郡|12=draft}}. * [[Keichō]] 7 (1602) – {{ill|Hosokawa Tadaoki|ja|細川忠興|12=draft}} begins constructing [[Kokura Castle]]. * [[Genna (era)|Genna]] 3 (1617) – The shrine is moved to its current location in Kokura Castle’s northern grounds as the clan guardian shrine. The southern and northern halls house deities transferred from Nagao and Sambommatsu (present-day {{ill|Tanga Market|ja|旦過市場|12=draft}}), respectively. * Genna 4 (1618) – Inspired by the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|ja|祇園祭|12=draft}} in Kyoto, Hosokawa introduces the Kokura Gion Festival, initially featuring decorated floats. * [[Meiji]] 6 (1873), May 15 – Designated a Ken-sha. * [[Shōwa]] 18 (1943), October 28 – The release of ''The Life of Matsu the Untamed'' popularizes the Kokura Gion Daiko nationally. * Shōwa 33 (1958), April 3 – Kokura Gion Daiko is designated an [[Important Intangible Folk Cultural Property]] by Fukuoka Prefecture. * Shōwa 62 (1987) – The current festival schedule is established. == Access == * A 4-minute (350 m) walk from [[Nishikokura Station]]. * Approximately 1.5 km from the [[Kitakyushu Urban Expressway]]’s {{ill|Ōtemachi Interchange|ja|大手町出入口|12=draft}} and 1.1 km from {{ill|Katsuyama Interchange|ja|勝山出入口|12=draft}}. == Nearby Attractions == * [[Kokura Castle]] * {{ill|Riverwalk Kitakyushu|ja|リバーウォーク北九州|12=draft}} * [[Kitakyushu City Hall]] * {{ill|Katsuyama Park (Kitakyushu)|ja|勝山公園 (北九州市)|12=draft}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Yasaka-jinja
